title: The dignity of jobs that no one applauds
summary: Argue that the dignity of invisible jobs is structural and must be built into pay, conditions, and daily treatment — not handed down in gestures.
topic: The dignity of jobs that no one applauds
prompt: Write a 350–500-word essay arguing for the dignity of jobs that no one applauds. Use two concrete examples and one counter-objection. Use cleft sentences, passive for topic-fronting, and concession.
hintStarters: What is recognised by a round of applause is not the job but the crisis that made it…
It is the night-shift cleaner, not the surgeon alone, who…
Granted, public gestures of thanks are not entirely without…
Real dignity is not handed down from a balcony; it is built into…

hintVocab.meaningVi: công việc vô hình / không được chú ý
phẩm giá mang tính cấu trúc
vỗ tay tán thưởng

được giữ sạch khuẩn / vô trùng
nhầm lẫn độ khó với tầm quan trọng
bị xem là đương nhiên
điều kiện làm việc
cái giá phải trả về thể chất
được coi trọng về mặt xã hội
khôi phục phẩm giá
ca đêm
bị trả lương thấp hơn xứng đáng
sự công nhận mang tính thể chế
làm trầm trọng thêm sự bất công
cử chỉ mang tính hình thức / màn trình diễn
nhân viên thông tin / hướng dẫn
được xây dựng vào trong / tích hợp trong

sampleText:
There is a reliable pattern in how societies respond to crises. When the infrastructure that holds daily life together becomes suddenly visible — during a pandemic, a transport strike, a power failure — people remember, briefly, that it exists. Night-shift cleaners in Manila's hospital wards were photographed and named during the worst weeks of the COVID-19 outbreak; the train-station information clerks at Kyoto's busiest stations, whose patient guidance keeps tens of thousands of confused travellers moving each day, were praised in online tributes when a service disruption made their work impossible to overlook. In both cases, the recognition lasted roughly as long as the emergency.

What is recognised by a round of applause is not the job but the crisis that made the job visible. This matters because the distinction reveals exactly what is being withheld in ordinary times. A cleaner who sanitises a surgical unit every four hours is not performing a task that is less consequential than the surgery that follows; it is precisely because the room is kept sterile that the surgery is possible. The value of that work does not fluctuate with public attention. What fluctuates is our willingness to see it.

Granted, gestures of public thanks are not entirely without value. Being named and photographed tells a worker that their effort was noticed, and that acknowledgement has real, if limited, meaning. It would be unkind to dismiss the applause from Manila's hospital balconies as worthless — the people who gave it meant something by it.

Even so, recognition of this kind does not address what actually determines whether a job carries dignity: the pay scale, the safety of the working conditions, the physical toll that shift work takes on a body over twenty years, and whether a worker is permitted to be exhausted without being judged as inadequate. An information clerk at a busy station who is paid too little to live near the station she serves, and who retires with permanent joint damage from standing on a hard floor for three decades, has not been dignified by the fact that passengers occasionally thanked her.

Real dignity is not handed down from a balcony. It is built into the structures that govern how a job is paid, scheduled, and respected in law and in the small, daily transactions of the workplace. Performative gestures, however warm, are a substitution for that structural work — and substitutions, however well-intentioned, compound the original injustice.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Cleft sentence with 'It is precisely because … that …'
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'it is precisely because the room is kept sterile that the surgery is possible' dùng cấu trúc nhấn mạnh 'It is … because … that …' để làm nổi bật mối quan hệ nhân quả giữa công việc của người lao công và kết quả phẫu thuật. 'Precisely' tăng cường thêm sức mạnh. Lưu ý rằng mệnh đề bị động 'the room is kept sterile' cũng đặt trọng tâm vào trạng thái (vô trùng) chứ không phải người thực hiện.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'it is precisely because the room is kept sterile that the surgery is possible' uses the 'It is … because … that …' cleft to highlight the causal link between the cleaner's work and the surgical outcome. 'Precisely' adds further force. Note that the passive 'the room is kept sterile' also foregrounds the state (sterility) rather than the agent.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive for topic-fronting in argument
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Night-shift cleaners in Manila's hospital wards were photographed and named' và 'It is built into the structures that govern how a job is paid, scheduled, and respected in law' đều dùng bị động để đặt chủ đề quan trọng vào đầu câu. Câu đầu đặt người lao công (không phải truyền thông hay xã hội) lên đầu; câu thứ hai đặt 'it' (= phẩm giá thực sự) lên đầu và ẩn đi chủ thể hành động để nhấn mạnh vào hệ thống.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Night-shift cleaners in Manila's hospital wards were photographed and named' and 'It is built into the structures that govern how a job is paid, scheduled, and respected in law' both use the passive to front the topic under discussion. The first places the workers (not the media or society) in subject position; the second foregrounds 'it' (= real dignity) and hides the agent to emphasise the systemic nature of the claim.

sampleTranslationVi:
Có một kiểu phản ứng quen thuộc của xã hội trước khủng hoảng. Khi cơ sở hạ tầng duy trì cuộc sống hàng ngày trở nên đột ngột nhìn thấy được — trong đại dịch, cuộc đình công giao thông, mất điện — mọi người nhớ, trong chốc lát, rằng nó tồn tại. Những người lao công ca đêm tại các phòng bệnh viện ở Manila được chụp ảnh và gọi tên trong những tuần tồi tệ nhất của dịch COVID-19; các nhân viên thông tin tại các ga tàu bận rộn nhất của Kyoto, những người có sự hướng dẫn kiên nhẫn giúp hàng chục nghìn hành khách bối rối di chuyển mỗi ngày, được khen ngợi trong các lời tri ân trực tuyến khi một sự gián đoạn dịch vụ khiến công việc của họ không thể bị bỏ qua. Trong cả hai trường hợp, sự ghi nhận kéo dài đại khái chừng nào cuộc khủng hoảng còn đó.

Điều được ghi nhận bởi một tràng vỗ tay không phải là công việc mà là cuộc khủng hoảng đã làm cho công việc trở nên nhìn thấy được. Điều này quan trọng bởi vì sự phân biệt này tiết lộ chính xác những gì đang bị giữ lại trong thời bình thường. Một người lao công khử trùng phòng phẫu thuật mỗi bốn giờ không thực hiện một công việc kém quan trọng hơn cuộc phẫu thuật theo sau; chính vì phòng được giữ vô trùng mà phẫu thuật mới có thể thực hiện được. Giá trị của công việc đó không dao động theo sự chú ý của công chúng. Điều dao động là sự sẵn lòng nhìn thấy nó của chúng ta.

Công nhận, những cử chỉ cảm ơn công khai không hoàn toàn vô giá trị. Được gọi tên và chụp ảnh cho người lao động biết rằng nỗ lực của họ đã được chú ý, và sự thừa nhận đó có ý nghĩa thực sự, dù hạn chế. Sẽ là không công bằng khi bác bỏ tiếng vỗ tay từ ban công bệnh viện ở Manila là vô nghĩa — những người vỗ tay đó có ý gì đó khi làm điều đó.

Dẫu vậy, sự ghi nhận loại này không giải quyết được điều thực sự quyết định liệu một công việc có mang lại phẩm giá hay không: thang lương, sự an toàn của điều kiện làm việc, cái giá về thể chất mà làm ca gây ra cho cơ thể trong suốt hai mươi năm, và liệu người lao động có được phép mệt mỏi mà không bị đánh giá là không đủ năng lực hay không. Một nhân viên thông tin tại ga bận rộn được trả quá ít để sống gần ga mà cô ấy phục vụ, và về hưu với tổn thương khớp vĩnh viễn do đứng trên sàn cứng trong ba thập kỷ, không được tôn nghiêm bởi việc hành khách đôi khi cảm ơn cô ấy.

Phẩm giá thực sự không được trao xuống từ ban công. Nó được xây dựng vào trong các cấu trúc chi phối cách một công việc được trả lương, sắp xếp và tôn trọng theo luật và trong các giao dịch hàng ngày nhỏ nhặt của nơi làm việc. Những cử chỉ mang tính hình thức, dù ấm áp đến đâu, là sự thay thế cho công việc cấu trúc đó — và sự thay thế, dù có thiện ý đến đâu, làm trầm trọng thêm sự bất công ban đầu.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a cleft structure with 'It is precisely because … that …' to highlight a causal link?
options: It is precisely because the room is kept sterile that the surgery is possible.
Because the room is kept sterile, precisely surgery is possible.
The room being kept sterile is precisely what makes surgery possibly happen.
It is the room that is kept sterile because surgery is precisely possible.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is precisely because … that …' is a cleft construction that places maximum stress on the causal relationship, emphasising that the cleaner's work is the direct prerequisite for the surgery.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'It is … because … that …' with the stressed cause placed after 'because'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses three passive verbs in parallel to describe what happened to workers?
options: Night-shift cleaners in Manila's hospital wards were photographed and named during the worst weeks of the COVID-19 outbreak.
Night-shift cleaners photographed and named themselves during the COVID-19 outbreak.
People photographed and named night-shift cleaners at Manila hospitals during COVID-19.
During COVID-19 night-shift cleaners, photographing and naming, were in Manila hospitals.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Were photographed and named' uses the past passive in parallel, placing the cleaners in subject position and foregrounding what was done to them rather than who did it.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option puts the workers (not the photographers or media) as the grammatical subject?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces a concession most naturally?
options: Granted, gestures of public thanks are not entirely without value.
Of course, public thanks is very valuable and everyone agrees on this.
Although public thanks is good, it is also true there are problems.
It is worth noting that public thanks have both positive and negative aspects.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' followed by 'not entirely without value' is precise hedged concession — it acknowledges the point without fully accepting it, preparing for the 'Even so' pivot.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'Granted' as a standalone opener and hedges with 'not entirely'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'what' cleft achieve in the sentence 'What is recognised by a round of applause is not the job but the crisis'?
options: It asks a question about recognition
It delays the key contrast ('not the job but the crisis') to the end for emphasis
It introduces a new topic about applause
It creates a passive construction about recognition
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What … is …' is a cleft that fronts the subject and places the key information — the contrast between 'the job' and 'the crisis' — at the end, where it carries most rhetorical weight.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about what part of the sentence gets the most emphasis and why.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a pivot back to the main argument after a concession?
options: In conclusion, gestures are also important.
Even so, recognition of this kind does not address what actually determines whether a job carries dignity.
On the contrary, public thanks mean absolutely nothing.
However all gestures are performative and therefore worthless.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' acknowledges the concession and pivots back, while 'does not address' introduces the key gap between gesture and structural dignity.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts what 'Granted' admitted and then restates the essay's main line of argument?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the difference between 'performative gesture' and 'structural dignity' as used in the essay?
options: Performative gestures are more lasting; structural dignity is temporary
Performative gestures are visible acts of thanks; structural dignity is built into pay, conditions, and legal protections
Structural dignity refers to public applause; performative gestures refer to pay increases
There is no meaningful difference — both terms describe the same thing
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly contrasts performative gestures (applause, naming, thanking) with structural dignity (pay scale, working conditions, legal respect), arguing the former is a 'substitution' for the latter.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the essay's definition of each term.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be used up' mean in the context of physical labour?
options: To be given too many tasks at once
To be fully consumed by a role and left exhausted or physically damaged over years
To use up all the available resources in a workplace
To be promoted out of a physically demanding job
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Used up' describes a person whose body or energy has been exhausted by sustained physical work — closely related to the essay's reference to 'the physical toll that shift work takes on a body over twenty years.'
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'used up' as a battery that has been drained completely.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the passive correctly to foreground the subject under discussion?
options: It is built into the structures that govern how a job is paid, scheduled, and respected in law.
The structures build into it how jobs are paid and scheduled and respected by laws.
Built into structures it is how a job pays, schedules, and respects in law.
Structures that govern are built into how jobs pay, schedule, and respect in law.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is built into …' uses a passive construction to present 'real dignity' (the 'it' referring back to the previous sentence) as the subject, emphasising what must be embedded in systems rather than who must act.
mcQuestions.hint: The passive here hides the agent and focuses attention on 'it' — real dignity — and where it must reside.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to mistake difficulty for importance' mean?
options: To assume that a difficult job is automatically more important than an easy one
To confuse two different types of work-related tasks
To underestimate how difficult a job is to learn
To overvalue jobs that require long training
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay implies that cleaning is easy to learn but critical in practice — society wrongly equates 'hard to learn' (like surgery) with 'more important', ignoring essential but simpler tasks.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about the surgical ward example: surgery is difficult, but without the cleaner's work it cannot safely happen.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains formal academic register throughout?
options: Cleaners and clerks do super important work and society basically ignores them which is really unfair.
Performative gestures, however warm, are a substitution for structural work — and substitutions, however well-intentioned, compound the original injustice.
Honestly invisible workers just get no respect and the pay is terrible and it needs to change now.
We all know that cleaners and station workers are important but nobody really does anything about it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses formal vocabulary ('performative', 'substitution', 'compound the original injustice'), a concessive 'however' clause, and a parallel final structure — all markers of B2+ academic prose.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'to be considered less important or significant than something else in practice'?
options: to compound an injustice
to be taken for granted
to restore dignity
to be underpaid
mcQuestions.explanation: 'To be taken for granted' means to have one's presence or contribution assumed and ignored — it captures exactly how invisible workers are treated in ordinary times.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase describes how people treat something they rely on but never consciously appreciate?

mcQuestions.prompt: According to the essay, why does applause at the time of a crisis fail to constitute real recognition?
options: Because applause is always insincere
Because recognition must be structural — embedded in pay, conditions, and law — not in temporary gestures
Because workers prefer pay rises to any form of public acknowledgement
Because crises are too short for recognition to develop
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that applause 'lasts roughly as long as the emergency' and does not address 'what actually determines whether a job carries dignity' — the structural conditions of pay, safety, and respect.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the first and final paragraphs together for the full argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause with 'whose' correctly to describe a worker?
options: The train-station information clerks at Kyoto's busiest stations, whose patient guidance keeps tens of thousands of confused travellers moving each day.
The train-station information clerks at Kyoto's busiest stations, that their patient guidance keeps tens of thousands moving.
The train-station information clerks at Kyoto's busiest stations, which patient guidance keeps tens of thousands moving.
The train-station information clerks at Kyoto's busiest stations, who their patient guidance tens of thousands keeps moving.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Whose' correctly expresses possession in a non-restrictive relative clause modifying a person (the clerks); 'whose patient guidance' means 'the patient guidance of the clerks'.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Whose' is the possessive relative pronoun for both people and things.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'compound the original injustice' mean in the essay's final sentence?
options: To solve the original injustice by addressing it step by step
To make the original injustice worse by adding something that appears helpful but is not
To document the original injustice for future reference
To recognise the original injustice through public ceremonies
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Compound' in this context means to make something worse by adding to it. Performative gestures that substitute for structural change make the underlying injustice worse, not better.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Compound an injury/problem' is a fixed expression meaning to increase its severity.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which of the following best paraphrases the essay's central argument?
options: Society should applaud invisible workers more often and more sincerely.
The dignity of invisible jobs depends on structural conditions — pay, safety, and respect in law — not on public gestures of thanks.
Invisible workers are more important than surgeons and other high-status professionals.
Public recognition is meaningless and workers should focus only on negotiating better wages.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay's core claim is that dignity is 'structural' and 'built into' systems, not 'handed down from a balcony' — public gestures are insufficient substitutions for this structural work.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the final paragraph for the essay's most direct statement of its argument.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option correctly uses 'however' as a concessive adverb inside a clause?
options: Performative gestures, however warm, are a substitution for that structural work.
However warm performative gestures, they are substitution for structural work.
Performative gestures however are warm a substitution for structural work.
However, warm performative gestures they are a substitution structural work.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'However + adjective' inserted between commas is the standard B2 pattern for an embedded concessive — 'however warm' means 'no matter how warm'. This is distinct from 'however' as a sentence-initial contrast marker.
mcQuestions.hint: In 'however + adjective' between commas, 'however' means 'no matter how'.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a job in your city that most people never think about but that would cause immediate disruption if it stopped. What would genuine structural recognition for that job actually look like — not as a gesture, but as policy?
